issues.body:
FULL_SELFTEST_SUITE is now derived by grepping scripts for the '--self-test' marker. The touched-file classifier _registry_selftest_targets (lines 794 and 799, unchanged context) emits a 'self:<base>' run ONLY when the script is a member of $suite. So suite membership — and therefore whether a touched script's --self-test is executed — now tracks the presence of the marker itself.

Consequence: a future PR that deletes or neuters a script's --self-test entrypoint (the classic 'weaken a self-test' attack) drops that script from the derived set. It is then excluded from BOTH the always-run regression suite (section 2, lines 1032-1042) and from touched-classification (section 1, only 'py:' compile remains). The registry-selftest gate stays green with its self-test coverage silently gone. Under the previous hard-coded list the script was still named, so 'python3 scripts/<x>.py --self-test' ran and, with the argument removed, argparse would exit non-zero -> die -> gate red. The static list acted as a tamper-EVIDENT anchor; the derivation makes marker removal self-consistent and undetectable at this gate. No committed manifest/baseline pins expected membership (confirmed by search), so nothing else re-establishes the invariant. For a trust plane whose charter is 'self-tests are the gate / never weaken a trust check', converting the gate from tamper-evident to self-consistent is a soundness regression, not merely a refactor.
issues.fix_hint: Preserve auto-enrollment AND tamper-evidence: keep a committed baseline set of scripts that MUST be self-tested (e.g. a checked-in expected-suite manifest, or the prior explicit list) and, after derivation, fail closed if any baseline member is absent from the derived suite (die: 'suite script <x> lost its --self-test'). That way new scripts still auto-enroll, but removing a known self-test marker turns the gate red instead of silently shrinking coverage. Add a fixture asserting that a baseline member whose marker was removed causes a fail-closed refusal.
issues.title: Deriving the suite from --self-test markers removes the static list's tamper-evidence: neutering a script's self-test now passes the gate silently

issues.body: _derive_full_selftest_suite uses 'grep -q -- \'--self-test\'', which matches the string anywhere in the file, including comments/docstrings. The added denylist fixture itself demonstrates this: denied.sh is enrolled solely because of the comment '# supports --self-test'. A script that merely documents --self-test in prose but does not implement the entrypoint would be enrolled and then invoked as 'python3 scripts/<x>.py --self-test' (or 'bash <x>.sh self-test'). Best case it fails closed (a legitimate PR unexpectedly red); worst case a script that tolerates unknown args runs its real main() under the self-test harness or increments the suite counter without asserting anything (a vacuous suite run). Not a security bypass, but a fragile heuristic for a trust-critical selector.
issues.fix_hint: Match an actual entrypoint pattern rather than any occurrence, e.g. require a dispatch line such as '\"--self-test\" in sys.argv', 'add_argument(\"--self-test\"', or the shell 'self-test' case — or grep for the invocation guard specifically — so a mere mention in a comment does not enroll a non-self-testing script.
issues.title: Marker match is purely textual, so a comment-only mention of --self-test enrolls (and then runs) a script

verdict.summary: The change replaces the hard-coded FULL_SELFTEST_SUITE with _derive_full_selftest_suite(), which greps scripts/*.py|*.sh for the literal '--self-test' and enrolls each match (minus an explicit, currently-empty denylist). Verified strengths: all 26 previously-listed scripts still contain the marker and stay enrolled (no silent drop today); four genuine self-tests are auto-enrolled (groom-alert.py, resolve-conflicts.py, retriage.py, curate-frontier.py) — a real coverage gain; the top-level call fails closed via die (defined at line 13) when the derived set is empty; the added fixtures are non-vacuous. Blocking concern (MAJOR): this is THE registry-selftest trust gate, and the touched-file classifier at lines 794/799 (unchanged context) emits a 'self:<script>' run ONLY for scripts that are members of the suite. Because membership is now derived from the '--self-test' marker itself, a future PR that removes/neuters a script's --self-test entrypoint silently drops it from BOTH the always-run suite (section 2, lines 1032-1042) AND touched-classification (section 1) — the gate stays green. Under the previous static list the same removal forced 'python3 scripts/<x>.py --self-test' to run and error (argparse exit 2) -> die -> gate red. No committed manifest pins expected membership, so the derivation is self-consistent and the automated 'self-tests are the gate' tamper-evidence for self-test neutering is lost. That erosion is exactly the never-weaken-a-trust-check property this cross-provider gate exists to protect, so I fail toward the maintainer. A secondary minor: the marker match is purely textual (the fixture's denied.sh matches on a bare comment), so a script that merely mentions --self-test in prose is enrolled and then run with --self-test.
